articles.title: Kids and Melatonin: A Sleep Aid Story
articles.summary: Melatonin is a popular sleep aid that many kids use to help them sleep better. It is often seen as a natural and easy solution for sleep problems. However, doctors are worried because we don't know enough about how safe it is for children in the long run. Some kids, especially those with conditions like autism, benefit from melatonin, but for other kids, the evidence is not strong. There are concerns about the doses in products, kids taking it without a doctor's advice, and accidental ingestions. This means we need to be more careful and have better rules for using melatonin with children. Sleep issues are common in kids and can affect their health and learning, so parents look for quick fixes, but melatonin is a hormone that does more than just help with sleep. More research is needed to understand its effects on kids' bodies over time.

articles.title: The Brightest Radio Flash Ever Found
articles.summary: Astronomers have found the brightest fast radio burst ever seen and discovered where it came from. Fast radio bursts are powerful flashes of radio energy from space that last only a short time. This new burst, called RBFLOAT, was detected on March 16, 2025, and lasted about one-fifth of a second. Using a network of telescopes called the CHIME/FRB Outrigger array, scientists pinpointed its origin to a galaxy named NGC 4141, which is about 130 million light-years away in the constellation Ursa Major. This galaxy is relatively close to Earth, making the burst appear very bright. The team also used the James Webb Space Telescope to look at the same spot and found a faint infrared signal, which might come from a star or be related to the burst. This discovery helps scientists learn more about these mysterious space events.

articles.title: Water Found Under Mars That Could Have Helped Life
articles.summary: Scientists have found clues that water once moved under the surface of Mars. This water could have helped tiny living things survive long ago. The study looked at old sand dunes in a place called Gale Crater on Mars, where NASA's Curiosity rover explores. The dunes turned into rock after water seeped into them from a nearby mountain. This water left behind minerals like gypsum, which can trap signs of life. The researchers think that even after Mars became dry on top, water kept moving underground, creating safe spots for microbes. This discovery helps us understand Mars better and shows where to look for past life. The work was done by scientists at New York University Abu Dhabi, who compared Mars rocks to similar ones in Earth's deserts.

articles.title: New Powers to Stop Illegal Dumping
articles.summary: The government in England is thinking about giving environment officers new powers to fight fly-tipping, which is when people dump waste illegally. These officers could search places without a warrant, take assets, and arrest suspects. Fly-tipping is already a crime, and people caught doing it might go to jail for up to five years. The Environment Agency has closed many illegal waste sites, but the government wants to do more. A plan to tackle waste crime will be released soon. Some people, like Tim Farron, support the idea but think it should be stronger. Drivers caught fly-tipping could get penalty points on their licenses, which might lead to losing their license. This illegal dumping costs a lot of money to clean up each year.

articles.title: Vitamin D and Long COVID: A Surprising Discovery
articles.summary: Scientists did a big study to see if vitamin D helps with COVID-19. They gave some people vitamin D pills and others fake pills. The vitamin D did not make COVID-19 less severe or stop people from going to the hospital. But they found something interesting about long COVID. Long COVID is when people still feel sick weeks after getting COVID-19, with problems like tiredness or trouble thinking. In the study, people who took vitamin D were a little less likely to have long COVID symptoms than those who took fake pills. The scientists say this is a clue that needs more research. The study was done in the United States and Mongolia with many adults. They hope to learn more to help people with long COVID.

articles.title: Local Council Gives Money for Green Projects
articles.summary: A local council in Staffordshire Moorlands is giving money to help communities fight climate change. People can get grants from £400 to £5,000 for projects like planting trees, improving transport, or recycling. The council has done this before, helping about 40 groups since 2021. Some projects include tree planting by a climate group and a green arts festival. The money helps people take action in their own neighborhoods. Applications will open in April and close in May, with more funding coming in the autumn. This is a way for local people to make their area greener and healthier.
